Raffles Girls' Primary School

2020 Primary 6 Science — Practice Paper (SA1, June)

Section A: 28 multiple-choice questions (56 marks) • Section B: 13 open-ended questions (44 marks) • Total 100 marks

Section A — Multiple Choice (Questions 1–28)

Question 1

2 marks

Study the flow chart below.

Which one of the following is classified correctly?

ABCD
(1)reptilefishamphibianbird
(2)mammalamphibianfishreptile
(3)insectreptilemammalbird
(4)mammalfishreptilebird

Answer

Option 4. Following the chart: "Does it have hair? Yes" → A = mammal (only mammals have hair/fur). For "No hair": "Does it have scales? Yes" then "Does it have gills? Yes" → B = fish (scales + gills). "Has gills? No" → C = reptile (scales but no gills). "Has scales? No" → D = bird. Only option 4 matches mammal / fish / reptile / bird.

Question 2

2 marks

Which one of the following animals is not an insect?

(1) mosquito    (2) dragonfly    (3) ant    (4) tick

Answer

Option 4. Insects have a body in 3 parts (head, thorax, abdomen) and 3 pairs of legs (6 legs). The mosquito (1), dragonfly (2) and ant (3) are all insects with 6 legs. The tick (4) has 8 legs and only 2 body parts — it is an arachnid, not an insect.

Question 3

2 marks

A, B, C and D are the stages of the life cycle of an insect.

At stage A, it does not feed, move or moult.

Which one of the following represents the stages of the life cycle of the insect?

ABCD
(1)egglarvapupaadult
(2)adultegglarvapupa
(3)pupaadultegglarva
(4)larvapupaadultegg

Answer

Option 3. The stage that "does not feed, move or moult" is the pupa, so A = pupa. Following the arrows around the cycle (pupa → adult → egg → larva → back to pupa): A = pupa, B = adult, C = egg, D = larva. This matches option 3.

Question 4

2 marks

Kumar placed an equal number of seeds of the same type in two identical beakers. Each beaker was exposed to a different set of conditions as shown below.

Kumar observed that the seeds in one of the beakers had germinated. Which one of the following explanations of Kumar's observation is correct?

BeakerObservationExplanation
(1)XSeeds germinated.Air, water and sunlight were present.
(2)XSeeds germinated.Air, water and warmth were present.
(3)YSeeds did not germinate.Light was absent.
(4)YSeeds did not germinate.Only water and warmth were present.

Answer

Option 2. Seeds need air, water and warmth to germinate (sunlight is not needed for germination). Beaker X was near a window (warm) with moist cotton and air, so its seeds germinated. Option 1 is wrong because sunlight is not required. Beaker Y was in the freezer — too cold — so it did not germinate, but options 3 and 4 give wrong reasons (the seeds failed because there was no warmth, not because of light or "only water and warmth").

Question 5

2 marks

The diagrams below show the plant and female human reproductive systems.

Which one of the following correctly identifies the parts where fertilisation takes place in the plant and human reproductive systems?

Plant Reproductive SystemHuman Reproductive System
(1)AC
(2)AD
(3)BC
(4)BD

Answer

Option 3. In the plant, fertilisation occurs in the ovary, found at the base of the pistil — labelled B. In the female human, fertilisation occurs in the oviduct (fallopian tube) — labelled C. So the answer is B and C.

Question 6

2 marks

The statements below describe the process of fertilisation at different stages.

A   The nuclei fuse.
B   A sperm enters the egg.
C   The fertilised egg divides.
D   Other sperms fail to enter the egg.
E   The sperms swim towards the egg.

Which one of the following identifies the correct arrangement of stages involved in fertilisation?

1st stagelast stage
(1)ACDED
(2)BADCE
(3)DBACE
(4)EBDAC

Answer

Option 4. The correct order is: E sperms swim towards the egg → B a sperm enters the egg → D other sperms then fail to enter → A the nuclei of sperm and egg fuse (this is fertilisation) → C the fertilised egg divides. This gives E, B, D, A, C — option 4.

Question 7

2 marks

The flow chart below shows some parts of the human body system(s) X. P and Q are substances found in the blood taken from the small intestine.

Which one of the following best represents P, Q and X?

Substance PSubstance QSystem(s) X
(1)oxygencarbon dioxiderespiratory
(2)carbon dioxideoxygencirculatory
(3)digested foodwaterdigestive and respiratory
(4)digested foodundigested fooddigestive and circulatory

Answer

Option 4. The flow chart (mouth → gullet → stomach → small intestine → large intestine → out of the body) is the digestive system. At the small intestine, P (digested food) is absorbed into the blood and carried to all parts of the body — this transport is done by the circulatory system. Q is the undigested food that passes on to the large intestine. So P = digested food, Q = undigested food, X = digestive and circulatory systems.

Question 8

2 marks

Benjamin placed four plants in identical jars, each containing water at the same level as shown below. He then placed the four set-ups, A, B, C and D, next to the window for an hour.

At the end of the experiment, Benjamin measured the height of the water level, h, in each jar. Which of the following correctly shows the height of water in set-ups A, B, C and D?

Set-up ASet-up BSet-up CSet-up D
(1)250195180170
(2)180170195250
(3)170180250195
(4)195250170180

Answer

Option 3. The more leaves a plant has, the more water it loses by transpiration, so the lower the water level. Set-up A has the most leaves → loses the most water → lowest level (170). Set-up B has fewer leaves (180). Set-up C has its roots wrapped in a plastic bag so it can hardly take up or lose water → highest level (250). Set-up D has a moderate number of leaves (195). This matches option 3: A 170, B 180, C 250, D 195.

Question 9

2 marks

The diagram below shows a plant cell.

The statements below are some statements about the parts of the above cell.

PartsFunctions
A   PControls all activities within the cell
B   QCaptures sunlight for plants to make food
C   RSupports and gives the cell its shape
D   SControls the movement of substances in and out of the cell.

Which of the following have parts that match with their functions correctly?

  • B and C only
  • A and D only
  • A, B and C only
  • A, B, C and D

Answer

Option 3. P is the nucleus — controls all activities (A correct). Q is the chloroplast — captures sunlight to make food (B correct). R is the cell wall — supports and gives the cell its shape (C correct). S is the cell wall region too, but the function "controls movement of substances in and out of the cell" belongs to the cell membrane, not S — so D is wrong. Hence A, B and C only.

Question 10

2 marks

The diagrams below show the human circulatory system and the plant transport system.

Which one of the following statements about the two systems is true?

  • Both break down food into simpler substances.
  • Both lose water in the form of water vapour only.
  • Both take in oxygen and give out carbon dioxide only.
  • Both transport nutrients and water to the different parts.

Answer

Option 4. The human circulatory system and the plant transport system both carry (transport) nutrients and water to the different parts of the organism. Option 1 is wrong — neither system digests/breaks down food. Options 2 and 3 describe gas exchange/transpiration, which is not the shared function of these two transport systems.

Question 11

2 marks

The diagrams below show how gases are transported in the blood through blood vessels P, Q, R, W, X, Y and Z in the circulatory systems of a fish and a man.

Based on the diagrams above, which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

A   W, X, and P carry blood rich in oxygen.
B   R, Q, Y and Z carry blood rich in carbon dioxide.
C   The heart is needed to pump oxygen from the gills to the body of the fish.

  • C only
  • A and B only
  • A and C only
  • A, B and C

Answer

Option 2. Vessels leaving the gas-exchange organ (gills in the fish, lungs in the human) carry oxygen-rich blood: P (from gills), and W, X. Vessels returning to the gas-exchange organ or coming from the body carry carbon-dioxide-rich blood: R, Q, Y and Z. So A and B are correct. C is wrong — the heart pumps blood, not oxygen, and in a fish the blood flows gills → body without returning through the heart first, so the heart does not pump oxygen "from the gills to the body".

Question 12

2 marks

Sarah wanted to find out if carbon dioxide is needed for photosynthesis. She prepared four set-ups, A, B, C and D, as shown below.

Which of the above set-ups should Sarah use to conduct her experiment?

  • A and C only
  • A and B only
  • B and C only
  • C and D only

Answer

Option 1 (A and C only). To test whether carbon dioxide is needed, the only difference between the two set-ups must be the presence/absence of carbon dioxide; everything else must be the same. Set-up A (clear glass box, carbon dioxide present) and Set-up C (clear glass box, substance removes carbon dioxide) differ only in carbon dioxide. Set-up D is painted black (changes light, a second variable) and Set-up B has no glass box, so they are not fair comparisons.

Question 13

2 marks

Wood is used as a building material to build houses as shown below.

Houses built using wood can withstand the force of strong wind and heavy rain. Why is this so?

  • Wood is flexible.
  • Wood is strong.
  • Wood is opaque.
  • Wood is able to float.

Answer

Option 2. A building material must be strong so the house can support its own weight and withstand the forces of strong wind and heavy rain without breaking. Being flexible, opaque or able to float are not the properties that let the house resist these forces.

Question 14

2 marks

A cube contains some water and air as shown in the diagram below.

Peter used a syringe to remove some air from the cube. Which one of the following shows the changes in the volume and mass of the air in the cube after some air has been removed?

Volume of airMass of air
(1)decreasedecrease
(2)decreaseremains the same
(3)remains the sameremains the same
(4)remains the samedecrease

Answer

Option 4. Air has no fixed shape and no fixed volume — it always fills up its container. After some air is removed, the remaining air still spreads out to fill the whole air space in the cube, so its volume remains the same. But because some air particles were taken out, there is less air, so its mass decreases.

Question 15

2 marks

Cheryl has a container filled with a mixture of two substances, X and Y. The table below shows the melting point and boiling point of the substances.

SubstanceMelting point (°C)Boiling point (°C)
X217700
Y420900

At what temperature should Cheryl heat the mixture such that one substance becomes a liquid and the other substance becomes a solid?

  • 300 °C
  • 500 °C
  • 800 °C
  • 900 °C

Answer

Option 1 (300 °C). A substance is liquid when the temperature is between its melting and boiling points, and solid below its melting point. At 300 °C: X (melts at 217, boils at 700) is above its melting point, so X is a liquid; Y (melts at 420) is below its melting point, so Y is a solid. This gives one liquid and one solid. At 500 °C both would be liquid; at 800 °C and 900 °C X would be a gas, so those are wrong.

Question 16

2 marks

The diagram below shows the arrangement of four bulbs, A, B, C and D, in a circuit.

Which one of the bulbs can be controlled by the switch?

  • A
  • B
  • C
  • D

Answer

Option 3 (C). A switch only controls the bulb(s) that are in the same branch (loop) as the switch, where opening the switch breaks the only path for current to that bulb. Bulb C is on the branch in series with the switch, so opening the switch cuts off current to C only. Bulbs A, B and D still have complete paths through the other branches, so they stay lit.

Question 17

2 marks

Study the circuit diagram below carefully.

Four objects, L, M, N and O, are connected to the circuit. One of them is a non-conductor of electricity while the others are conductors of electricity. When the switch is closed, only two bulbs light up.

Which one of the following objects is a non-conductor of electricity?

  • L
  • M
  • N
  • O

Answer

Option 3 (N). Each object sits in series with a bulb on its own branch. A non-conductor breaks its branch so that bulb cannot light. Since only two of the bulbs light up, the branches containing the non-conductor stay off. Tracing the circuit, object N is the one whose branch (and the bulb sharing its path) does not light — it is the non-conductor, while L, M and O conduct and complete their branches.

Question 18

2 marks

Wendy hung four magnets, P, Q, R and S, above a tray of identical iron pins. Her observation is shown below.

Which of the following statements are correct?

A   Magnet S is the strongest magnet.
B   Magnet P is weaker than Magnet R.
C   Magnet R is stronger than Magnet Q.
D   Both Magnets P and Q have the same strength.

  • A and B only
  • B and D only
  • A, B and C only
  • A, C and D only

Answer

Option 1 (A and B only). The stronger the magnet, the more iron pins it attracts. From the diagram, S attracts the most pins → S is the strongest (A correct). P attracts fewer pins than R → P is weaker than R (B correct). Comparing R and Q, R does not attract more than Q (so C is wrong), and P and Q do not attract the same number (so D is wrong). Hence only A and B are correct.

Question 19

2 marks

Peter cycles along the path shown below.

Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

A   He lowers his body and head to reduce air resistance in order to cycle faster.
B   He lowers his body and head to increase air resistance in order to cycle faster.
C   He finds it harder to cycle up slope because he is moving against the direction of gravity.
D   He finds it harder to cycle up slope because he is moving in the same direction of gravity.

  • A only
  • B only
  • A and C only
  • B and D only

Answer

Option 3 (A and C only). Lowering the body and head makes the cyclist more streamlined, which reduces air resistance and helps him go faster — so A is correct and B is wrong. Going up a slope is harder because he moves against the pull of gravity (he has to do work to rise) — so C is correct and D is wrong.

Question 20

2 marks

Marcus attached each identical wooden block to a small container. Next, he placed the wooden blocks on four different surfaces labelled M, N, P and Q. He added 10 g-weights one by one into each container until the wooden block attached started to slide across the surface. He recorded the results in the table below.

SurfaceNumber of 10 g-weights required for block to start sliding
M8
N2
P10
Q5

Based on the information above, which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

A   Surface M is smoother than P but rougher than N and Q.
B   Most gravitational force is acting on the block sliding on surface P.
C   Frictional force between the wooden block and surface had to be overcome before it started sliding.
D   The minimum amount of weights required to move the wooden block on surface N is 20 g.

  • A only
  • B only
  • B and C only
  • A, C and D only

Answer

Option 4 (A, C and D only). More weights needed means more friction, i.e. a rougher surface. Order: P (10) > M (8) > Q (5) > N (2). So M is smoother than P but rougher than N and Q — A correct. Friction had to be overcome before sliding began — C correct. On surface N, 2 ten-gram weights = 20 g are needed — D correct. B is wrong: the gravitational force (weight) acting on the identical blocks is the same on every surface; the difference is friction, not gravity.

Question 21

2 marks

Catherine dropped two balls, M and N, of the same size into a tray of flour from the same height as shown below. Ball M has a greater mass than ball N.

She recorded the depth of the dent made by the balls in the tray of flour in the table below.

1st try2nd try3rd tryAverage
M33.53.53.33
N????

Based on the information above, which of the following statement(s) is/are definitely correct?

A   More frictional force was acting on M than N.
B   More gravitational force was acting on M than N.
C   The average depth of the dent made by ball N would be less than 3.33 cm.

  • B only
  • C only
  • A and B only
  • B and C only

Answer

Option 4 (B and C only). Ball M has a greater mass, so a greater gravitational force (weight) acts on it — B is correct. Because M hits the flour with more force, it makes a deeper dent, so the lighter ball N must make a shallower dent than M's average of 3.33 cm — C is correct. A is wrong: the balls are the same size and fall through the same flour and air, so the frictional/air resistance on them is not necessarily different — it cannot be said M definitely had more friction.

Question 22

2 marks

Study the diagram below. Four children and two of their toys are separated by screens, A, B, C, D and E.

Given that the screens are made of different materials, the following results were recorded:

• Tom is unable to see Alice.
• Lily is unable to see the toy truck.
• Alice can see both the teddy bear and the toy truck.

Based on the information above, which one of the following could possibly be the materials that have been used to make the screens?

ABCDE
(1)clear plasticmetalclear plasticwoodclear glass
(2)woodclear plasticclear glassmetalclear plastic
(3)clear plasticclear glassmetalwoodclear glass
(4)metalmetalwoodclear plasticclear glass

Answer

Option 2. "Cannot see through" needs an opaque screen (wood/metal); "can see through" needs a transparent screen (clear plastic/glass). Tom cannot see Alice → a screen between them is opaque. Lily cannot see the toy truck → a screen there is opaque. Alice can see both the teddy bear and the toy truck → the screens between Alice and those toys are transparent. Only option 2 places opaque screens (wood at A, metal at D) where vision is blocked and transparent screens (clear plastic/glass at B, C, E) where the children can see through.

Question 23

2 marks

Gary cut out holes of different shapes and sizes in the centre of three squares, X, Y and Z, which are made of plastic, wood and tracing paper respectively.

Next, Gary shone light on the three shapes using the set-up below. The three shapes are placed at different distances from the torch.

The diagrams below show what was seen on the screen. Which one of the following shadows is most likely to be formed on the screen?

Answer

Option 2. Plastic (X) and wood (Y) are opaque, so light only passes through their cut-out holes, forming bright star and diamond shapes on a dark shadow. Tracing paper (Z) is translucent, so it lets some light through everywhere and casts only a faint, blurred round patch rather than a sharp bright circle. Option 2 correctly shows the sharp star and diamond of light from the opaque squares with the dim, fuzzy circle from the translucent tracing paper. (Options showing a sharp bright circle treat tracing paper as if it had a clear hole, which is wrong.)

Question 24

2 marks

Natalie wanted to make a shirt to keep her warm on cold days. She wrapped four materials, A, B, C and D, around each identical container covered with a lid. Each container was filled with the same amount of hot water as shown below.

Natalie recorded the temperature of the water at the start of the experiment and twenty minutes later.

Time (min)Material AMaterial BMaterial CMaterial D
060606060
2032403836

Based on the results, which cloth material should Natalie choose for making the shirt?

  • A
  • B
  • C
  • D

Answer

Option 2 (B). A shirt that keeps her warm should be the best heat insulator — it should lose heat the slowest, keeping the water warmest. After 20 minutes, Material B's water is the hottest (40 °C, the smallest temperature drop), so B is the best insulator and the best choice. Material A cooled the most (down to 32 °C), so it is the poorest insulator.

Question 25

2 marks

Kenneth attached two cups, W and X, on a balanced rod as shown below.

He placed a candle below Cup W and observed the following ten minutes later.

Three of his classmates gave the following explanations for the above observations:

Alex : Cup X is made of a better conductor of heat.
Betty : The air above the candle flame gained heat.
Cody : The air above the candle flame rose.

Which of his classmates correctly explained the observation?

  • Betty only
  • Cody only
  • Alex and Betty only
  • Betty and Cody only

Answer

Option 4 (Betty and Cody only). The candle heats the air above it, so the air gains heat (Betty correct). Warm air is lighter and rises, pushing up Cup W so that side of the rod goes up (Cody correct). Alex is wrong — the tilt is caused by warm air rising and pushing the cup, not by how well Cup X conducts heat.

Question 26

2 marks

Serene placed a steel clip between 2 electrical circuits, A and B, as shown in the diagram below.

When Serene closed switches S1 and S2 at the same time, which one of the following observations would she make? The steel clip would ____________.

  • be attracted to the iron bar
  • be attracted to the copper bar
  • remain in its original position
  • be attracted to the copper bar and then to the iron bar

Answer

Option 1 (be attracted to the iron bar). When current flows, the coil around the iron bar turns it into an electromagnet, but copper is non-magnetic, so the coil around the copper bar does not make it a magnet. With only the iron bar becoming magnetic, the steel clip is attracted to the iron bar.

Question 27

2 marks

Denny was playing on a swing as shown in the diagram below.

Which one of the following statements is true when Denny swung from position A to B and then to C?

  • The potential energy at A, B and C are the same.
  • Potential energy was the highest at A and was lost at B.
  • Kinetic energy increased from A to B and again from B to C.
  • Kinetic energy increased from A to B and decreased from B to C.

Answer

Option 4. A and C are the high points of the swing and B is the lowest. Swinging down from A to B, height drops so potential energy converts to kinetic energy — kinetic energy increases. Swinging up from B to C, the swing rises and slows, so kinetic energy converts back to potential energy — kinetic energy decreases. Option 4 matches. (Option 1 is wrong because PE differs with height; option 3 is wrong because KE does not keep increasing; option 2 wrongly says PE is "lost".)

Question 28

2 marks

The picture below shows a solar panel which is found on the roof of a house. The solar panel is connected to the water heater in the bathroom.

Which of the following shows the correct energy conversion, taking place from the solar panel to the water heater?

  • potential energy → light energy → heat energy
  • light energy → electrical energy → heat energy
  • kinetic energy → electrical energy → heat energy
  • electrical energy → chemical energy → heat energy

Answer

Option 2. The solar panel receives light energy from the Sun and converts it into electrical energy. This electrical energy flows to the water heater, which converts it into heat energy to warm the water. So the chain is light → electrical → heat energy.

Section B — Open-Ended (Questions 29–41)

Question 29

3 marks

Sam conducted a field study on the seed dispersal of plants P and Q. He recorded his observations on the distribution of seeds by the plants in the diagram below.

Key
Pparent plant P
Qparent plant Q
• (dots)plant P
▲ (triangles)plant Q
arrowwind direction
(a) State the method of dispersal of plant P and Q. [1]
(i) P: __________    (ii) Q: __________
(b) Give a reason for your answer in (a)(i). [1]
(c) State one physical characteristic the fruit of Q is most likely to have that helps in its dispersal. [1]

Answer

(a) (i) P: by splitting (mechanical/explosive dispersal).   (ii) Q: by animals (or by wind).
(b) The seeds/plants of P are scattered very close to (clustered around) the parent plant P, which shows the fruit burst open and flung the seeds only a short distance — typical of dispersal by splitting.
(c) If dispersed by animals: the fruit is fleshy / brightly coloured / has hook-like structures so it sticks to or is eaten by animals. (If dispersed by wind: it has wing-like structures.)

Question 30

3 marks

The graph below shows the amount of undigested food as it goes through the different parts of the digestive system (A, B, C, D, E).

(a) Based on the graph, in which part of the digestive system, A, B, C or D, was the greatest amount of food digested? Explain your answer. [2]
(b) Which part of the graph represents the large intestine? Give a reason for your answer. [1]

Answer

(a) Part D. In part D there is the greatest decrease in the amount of undigested food, which means the most food was digested there. (Part D is the small intestine, where digestion takes place at the greatest rate.)
(b) Part E. In part E the amount of undigested food is the least and stays the same (the line is flat) — no further digestion occurs. No digestion takes place in the large intestine, so part E represents the large intestine.

Question 31

4 marks

Peter conducted an experiment shown below in a dark room. He then repeated his experiment by adding some water snails.

Distance of lamp from water plant (cm)Without water snailWith water snails
51619
101114
15610
2025
(a) In the absence of a water snail, the number of bubbles produced decreases as the distance from the lamp increases. Explain why. [1]
(b) Explain why there was an increase in the number of bubbles produced when water snails were present. [2]
(c) Peter conducted the experiment in a dark room. Give a reason why this helped to make the experiment a fair test. [1]

Answer

(a) As the lamp is moved further away, the water plant receives less light. This lowers the rate of photosynthesis, so the plant produces less oxygen — fewer bubbles per minute.
(b) The water snails give out carbon dioxide during respiration. With more dissolved carbon dioxide in the water, the water plant can carry out photosynthesis at a higher rate, so it produces more oxygen bubbles.
(c) Doing it in a dark room prevents any outside light from reaching the plant. This makes sure the number of bubbles produced depends only on the light from the lamp, keeping the test fair.

Question 32

4 marks

Kennis used the set-up below to find out whether water plants affect the amount of carbon dioxide in water at different times of the day. She placed the set-up near the window and added a few drops of a liquid X to the water.

The table below shows how Liquid X changes colour as it interacts with different concentrations of carbon dioxide in the water.

Amount of carbon dioxide in waterLess than normalNormalHigher than normal
Colour of water with liquid XPurpleRedYellow
(a) In the table below, write the colour of water with liquid X at noon and at midnight. [2]
Time of the dayAt noonAt midnight
Colour of water with liquid X
(b) Explain your answer provided for "at midnight" in (a) clearly. [2]

Answer

(a) At noon: purple  |  At midnight: yellow
(b) At midnight there is no sunlight, so the water plant cannot photosynthesise and does not take in carbon dioxide. The plant only respires — taking in oxygen and giving out carbon dioxide. This makes the amount of carbon dioxide in the water higher than normal, turning the water with liquid X yellow.

Question 33

4 marks

The device below is used in some countries to obtain pure drinking water from the surrounding air. The solar panel, which is attached to the fan, powers the fan. Air from the surroundings is drawn underground through the slanted pipes when the fan rotates. Pure drinking water obtained by this method can be pumped above the ground with the help of the pump attached.

(a) The temperature of air above the ground ranges from 18 °C to 46 °C while the temperature underground ranges from 7 °C to 18 °C. Explain how water can be obtained from the air that passes through the pipes. [2]
(b) One way to collect more water using this device is to pass more air through the inlet. Suggest two other changes to the device that would enable it to collect more water over a fixed period of time. [2]

Answer

(a) The warm, moist air from above the ground comes into contact with the much cooler inner surface of the underground pipe. The water vapour in the air loses heat to the cool pipe and condenses into water droplets, which collect as liquid water.
(b) Any two of: increase the surface area of the solar panel (or add more solar panels) so the fan runs faster and draws in more air; increase the length of the pipe so the air has more time/surface to cool and condense; bury the pipes deeper underground so they are even colder, causing more condensation.

Question 34

4 marks

A beaker of ice was heated and the change in temperature was recorded in the graph below (parts X, Y, Z).

(a) Name the processes that are represented by parts X and Z of the graph. [2]
X
Z
(b) Fill in the table below with a (√) against the parts X, Y, Z to indicate if there is heat gain as water changes from one state to another. [1]
PartsHeat gain
X
Y
Z
(c) The burner was not switched off throughout the experiment. Suggest one reason why there was a decrease in temperature after the 8th minute. [1]

Answer

(a) X: Melting (the ice at 0 °C turns to water — temperature stays constant).   Z: Boiling (the water at 100 °C turns to steam — temperature stays constant).
(b) X: √   Y: √   Z: √ — heat is gained throughout, as the substance is being heated the whole time (used to change state in X and Z, and to raise the temperature in Y).
(c) More ice (or cold water) had been added to the beaker, so the heat gained went into warming the added ice/water, causing the temperature to drop even though the burner was still on.

Question 35

3 marks

The circuits below are set up with different materials E, F, G and H.

The results of the 3 set-ups, A, B and C, are shown in the table below.

Set-upDoes the bulb light up?
ANo
BYes
CYes
(a) Will the bulb light up in Set-up D? Explain your answer. [2]

The materials E, F, G and H are connected in another circuit as shown below.

(b) In the circuit diagram above, write E, F, G and H in the correct box so that only three bulbs in the circuit will light up. [1]

Answer

(a) No, the bulb will not light up. From the results: A (E + G) does not light, but B (E + H) lights, so E is a conductor and G is the non-conductor (insulator). C (H + F) lights, so F is also a conductor. Set-up D contains G, and since G is an insulator no electricity can flow through it — this forms an open circuit, so the bulb in Set-up D stays unlit.
(b) Place the insulator G in the lower-branch box between bulbs B4 and B5. Put conductor E in the top box (in series with B1), H in the bottom-left box and F in the right-hand vertical box (as shown in the official answer key). G breaks the B3–B4 branch, so B3 and B4 stay off, while current still flows through the top branch (B1) and through the B2 branch to B5. Only B1, B2 and B5 light up — exactly three bulbs.

Question 36

3 marks

Chris placed 3 ring magnets through a plastic rod. Each ring magnet has an N-pole and an S-pole. The reference magnet A is shown below (N-pole shaded on top).

(a) Shade and label the N-pole of the magnets B and C in the diagram below. [1]
(b) What should Chris do to make magnet A "float" above magnet B? Explain your answer. [2]

Answer

(a) Magnet A floats because its bottom face (S-pole) repels magnet B's top face — so B must have its N-pole at the bottom. Magnet C is then shaded with its N-pole at the top, so that B's bottom (N) faces C's top (N) and they repel too. The shaded N-poles are shown below.
(b) Chris should flip magnet A over so that the N-pole of magnet A faces the N-pole of magnet B (like poles facing each other). Since like poles repel, A and B push each other apart, allowing magnet A to "float" above magnet B.

Question 37

3 marks

James carried out an experiment on two different toy helicopters, A and B, using the set-up shown below. He launched helicopter A at an angle from point X. Its results are shown below.

AttemptsDistance (cm)
1st330
2nd370
3rd350
(a) Give a possible reason why the distance moved by helicopter A was different for each attempt. [1]
(b) Name two forces that were acting on the helicopter when it was moving. [1]
(c) The average distance moved by the 15 g toy helicopter A is 350 cm. If James launched a 35 g toy helicopter B in the same direction with the same force, draw the path of toy helicopter B on the diagram above using the same starting point at X. [1]

Answer

(a) He launched helicopter A at a different angle (or with a slightly different amount of force) for each attempt, so the distance it travelled was different each time.
(b) Gravitational force (the weight pulling the helicopter down) and air resistance / frictional force between the helicopter and the air.
(c) Helicopter B is heavier (35 g vs 15 g) but launched with the same force, so it travels a shorter distance and a lower path, landing before point Y. The path is drawn in red below.

Question 38

3 marks

Peter wanted to find out how the surface area of a parachute affected the time taken for him to run five metres with it. The diagram below shows Peter running with the parachute.

Surface area of parachute (cm²)Time taken to complete five metres (s)
90020
100028
110034
120045
130059
(a) Based on the information above, what is the relationship between the surface area of the parachute and the time taken to complete the five-metre run? [1]

Peter cut a few holes in the 1300 cm² parachute and then ran with it.

(b) Would the time recorded for Peter to complete running 5 metres be 'more than', 'less than' or 'the same' as 59 seconds? Explain your answer clearly. [2]

Answer

(a) As the surface area of the parachute increases, the time taken to complete the five-metre run also increases.
(b) Less than 59 seconds. Cutting holes in the parachute reduces the surface area facing the air, so there is less air resistance acting against Peter. With less air resistance slowing him down, Peter can run faster and so completes the 5 metres in a shorter time than before.

Question 39

3 marks

Jerry was watching television at position B in a dark room as shown below.

(a) Why was Jerry able to see the television screen in the dark? [1]

Tom measured the length of the shadow cast on the wall as Jerry moved from Position A to D.

(b) In the table below, write the length of the shadow when Jerry was at Position C. [1]
Jerry’s positionLength of shadow cast (cm)
A180
B164
C
D104
(c) Describe where Jerry must be positioned in order to cast a shadow of about 170 cm long. [1]

Answer

(a) Light from the television screen travelled (was emitted) and reached Jerry's eyes, allowing him to see the screen even in the dark. (The TV is a light source.)
(b) About 130 cm. As Jerry moves further from the TV (light source) towards the wall, his shadow gets shorter (A 180 → B 164 → D 104), so position C lies between B and D at roughly 130 cm.
(c) A 170 cm shadow is between the values at A (180 cm) and B (164 cm), so Jerry must stand between Position A and Position B (closer to A).

Question 40

3 marks

Ahmad set up the experiment shown below to find out the heat conductivity of four different materials, A, B, C and D. An iron pin is held by wax on each material, and a heater is placed below.

MaterialTime taken for iron pin to drop (min)Put a cross (X)
A13
B9
C15
D2
(a) Ahmad's sister said that he had recorded ONE of the results wrongly. Put ONE cross (X) in the table above to indicate the mistake he had made. [1]
(b) Explain your answer in part (a). [1]
(c) Ahmad's sister also said that he did not conduct a fair test. Suggest what Ahmad can do to the set-up to ensure a fair test. [1]

Answer

(a) The cross (X) should be placed against Material C (time = 15 min).
MaterialTime taken (min)Put a cross (X)
A13
B9
C15X
D2
(b) Material C is the second nearest material to the heat source. It should gain heat faster than A and B, so its wax should melt sooner and the pin should drop in a shorter time than A and B — not the longest time (15 min). So the 15-minute result for C must be wrong.
(c) Ahmad should make sure each material is the same distance from the heat source (e.g. heat each material separately, or give each its own identical heater placed the same distance away), so that distance from the heater is the same for all materials.

Question 41

4 marks

Su Min conducted an experiment using a wound-up toy car. She wound up the toy car by turning the key and recorded the distance it travelled on the floor before coming to a complete stop.

Number of turns of keyDistance travelled (cm)
23
46
69
812
(a) State the energy conversion of the wound-up toy car when it was released, in the boxes provided. [1]
(b) What is the relationship between the number of turns of the key and the distance travelled by the toy car? [1]
(c) Using the same toy car and floor surface, suggest one change Su Min could make to the car to enable it to travel a further distance. [1]
(d) Explain why the toy car stopped moving after travelling a distance. [1]

Answer

(a) Elastic potential energy → Kinetic energy + Sound and Heat energy. The wound-up spring stores elastic potential energy, which converts to kinetic energy (movement), with some lost as sound and heat.
(b) As the number of turns of the key increases, the distance travelled by the toy car also increases. (Each 2 extra turns adds 3 cm.)
(c) She could turn the key a greater number of times than before, so the spring stores more elastic potential energy and the car travels further.
(d) All the kinetic energy of the toy car had been converted (mainly to sound and heat energy as it overcame friction), so the car had no more kinetic energy left to keep moving and it stopped.
